speaker: interviewer
text: Today's December 11, 1996. And this is number five of my interview with Eva Lax Slomovits. What do you remember about the-- about the ride from Mukacevo.

speaker: interviewee
text: Once-- once we were on the train, they closed the trains. And there were just little windows. And everybody was trying to get some air from the windows. And the people were praying and crying. And babies were crying. Some babies really got sick. I had a little cousin, that she was named after my grandma. Her name was [? Rochella. ?] And she only wanted I should hold her. She was so sick, so hot. She was running a fever. And she had-- she-- she was so-- [? Avi, ?] hold me. [? Avi, ?] hold me. Give me water. Give me water. We didn't have any water to give the sick babies. And it was very difficult. There was-- we were depressed. And it was so, so terrible. It's crowded. We were just sitting one next to the other. Or, some people would stand up, so the other people could sit or lean against, sleep a little bit. And then, every time the-- the train would pass by stations, they would whistle. So we-- we knew we'll be going, you know, what stations we go.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we came to a city, [INAUDIBLE]. It was a big city already there. So the train stopped, and they opened up the doors from the train in [INAUDIBLE]. And we saw the German soldiers and-- and the Hungarian. And they were giving them some papers. And the German soldiers brought water, pails of water, and put it in the train.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said, gee, that's great. We have water. And the Germans treat us better than the Hungarians. I says, they give us at least some water for the kids. And I says, please. Give me some water for the baby. Give me, Rochella's so sick. I gave her some water. And she was so happy that I gave her some water. And then, they closed the trains again. And we were going and going and going on this train. And-- and then, all of a sudden, somebody came out-- was standing near the window and says, you know, we just passed the-- the-- the-- the Polish border. We're already in Poland. They saw the Polish stations, the names. And I-- now, my father says we lost. He gave us always hope. But he just felt that if we're in Poland, that's the end. But he-- he took us together, the sisters and my brothers. And he said to us, you remember for-- I had $600 that I-- I had for the boat, the ticket. I didn't give the $600 when they-- we came into the ghetto. So I have that money. And everybody has some money in their coat. So he did it on his own. He put in in the-- in the shoulder pad, he put in some money for us. He says, a-- an American dollar is always good. If you could buy yourself some bread, if you could run away, if you could do something, you have some money with you. But he says, you should try everything to survive if you can. But he says, if you have to die, [SOBBING] you should know that you're dying, because you Jewish. You're not dying that you did something wrong, only because you're Jews. There are bad people that chose to be bad, to kill Jewish people. But he says, if you could save yourself, you should do everything in your power to survive and to continue to be Jewish and to continue the-- the life that we-- I started you out with. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we were crying. And-- And then, we were riding the train and everybody was praying. And then, all of a sudden, the train stopped. It stopped. They said, it's Auschwitz. We never heard of Auschwitz. We never heard of a camp. And everybody says, this, this. Well, my father looked out from the window. And he says, you know, they look-- these people look like they're working here. They all wear the same clothes-- striped clothes. Men. He didn't see any women. But he says, I see men. And it looks like they're working here. So he says, maybe we have a chance.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we work. So he says, work is not going to kill us. [? This one, ?] they're not going to kill us. He says that you have to work hard. You work hard. He says, make sure that you work hard just to survive.

speaker: interviewee
text: And while he was talking to us, they opened up the trains. They opened the trains. And there were standing Germans with German shepherds-- dogs. And they-- all of a sudden they said, we have to separate man and woman. They took like-- there was such a confusion getting off the train and such a screams that we-- we didn't know where to go. And somebody says, you know, these people go home. And these people, because they're young, they go to work. I had the baby, the [NON-ENGLISH], in my hand. And I figure, oh, I could go home with her, because I saw the women with the children go to-- to the left. And the working people go to the right, the young people. I held this little girl. And I was going to the left. And then, a man came over. He was a Jewish man with the striped clothes. He took away the baby from me. And he says, you have to go there. I says, no. I have to go home. He says, you just go there. And he took the baby. He threw her between the luggage. There was a lot of luggage that people took off. And this little girl starts screaming. [SOBBING] [? Avi, ?] she called me [? Avi. ?] She couldn't say, Eva.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she says [? Avi. ?] [? Avi. ?] And she was screaming. And I-- I didn't want to go. I want to go back for the baby. And he was pushing the other way.

speaker: interviewee
text: He already, this man, knew where those people go. He wanted to save me. So here, I lost already-- my sisters already left before. I lost my two sisters. I was already between strangers myself. They always took a hundred people, lined them up, five. And they took a hundred people, and they walked them away.

speaker: interviewee
text: So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was there-- I still had friends and cousins that I was together with them. When-- when they were separated with my father, my father, my brothers, my aunts with the children left to the left. And here, I wound up to the right, because they took away the baby from me. So I was already by myself without my sisters. So I says, if I just could find my sisters, to be with my sisters. But you couldn't go, because every time they counted a hundred, they would take them. So here, they took us to this-- it was a place-- a big place.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I found my sisters there. There-- still-- we still had our clothes on, our hair. And I found my sisters. I says, oh, I'm so happy that I found my sisters. And we have to work. We'll work, go to work together.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they lined us up. And they said we should get undressed. And we should put something-- tie our clothes, then we should know the-- the belongings, which belonging-- always with tricks.

speaker: interviewee
text: We took our clothes. We got undressed. We tied our clothes. We put it in a bundle. And they told us to walk. We walked out from that room, and there were men. They were from camp, Jewish men. And they were start cutting our hair. We were so embarrassed in front of man. [SOBBING] We didn't know where to put our hands, where to cover ourselves. We never stood naked in front of other people. It was so difficult. They cut our hair. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were without clothes. And they told us to walk after they cut our hair. Told us to walk. We walked in into a room. And they gave us a shower. And they gave us-- we walked out from the shower. They gave us one dress. Everybody got the same dress, a gray dress. I was lucky I was small, so the dress fitted me. But if some people were big, the dress didn't fit them. And they went back to change the dress, they would hit them. And they gave us wooden shoes. And they lined us up, again, five. Again, a hundred. And they walked us. This was in Birkenau. They walked us into a-- a block. It was called-- it was a big building. And this building was-- the middle. It was, for some reason, was divided. Then, I found out that-- that that division was-- in the wintertime, they build it to heat it. But they never heated the barracks So it was like three-- three cubbies. It was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Three levels?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right, three levels. And on each level-- each cubby, 10 people went in. So five people lay down this way and five people this way. It was impossible for 10 people to lay down.

speaker: interviewee
text: So here we are. They gave us a blanket. And here we are. And-- and they say in Auschwitz. We didn't know what it was. [INAUDIBLE] We start asking the girls, what is here? Are we-- are we going to work here? What is it? What is here? So there were these girls from Slovakia. And they were in charge on the-- on the-- the barrack. So they says, you think you have it bad?

speaker: interviewee
text: When we came, we didn't have barracks. We build these barracks. They says, you have already good.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I says, where we'll be able to see the-- our parents, the children? Where will be able to-- what is here? So they says, look. We going to tell you as it is. You see that smoke? That's your parents. And don't ever think that you're going to see them. Oh, my god. We started to cry. Says, you're crazy. How could you be so cruel? [SOBBING] How could you tell us something like that? How could you be so cruel? It's not true. We didn't want to accept that what-- for reality. [CRYING] So next day, in the morning we went out roll call. It was called [NON-ENGLISH]. They didn't give us any food, nothing that day. So they called in the morning [NON-ENGLISH]. We was roll call. And we start asking other people there, what is here? Tell us. Is it true? We already smell the smoke and the flesh. And we still didn't want to believe. So there was this-- this gypsies. They came. Oh, they start giving-- they gave us coffee while we were on [NON-ENGLISH]. So the gypsies came. They knew already that we would drink it. We would eat it. So they came to take it from us. We gave it to them. So-- the-- this was a very tough thing to believe. So when we came into the barracks, they gave us lunch, soup. And each-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] and each cubby, they would get one plate. So they says, where are the spoons? They says, what spoons? You drink from eat-- everybody takes a sip. I said, how could that be? But you know, we were so hungry already, so thirsty, that we say, we better give in. We better start drinking. So we already ate. We drink that soup. And we were only three days in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: After three days, they came again. They didn't select us, nothing. They just counted a certain amount of people. And they took us back to a train. But this train wasn't so crowded anymore. They-- they put about a hundred girls on one train. And we-- we went about three hours on that train. And we got to Krakau in Poland, a city. We saw the station's Krakau. We got off this train. And we walked to Plaszow. We came there. It was a camp. Before we came to this camp, it was a ghetto. It was on the cemetery from Krakau [INAUDIBLE] that-- that camp. And before we came, there were children in this camp. And they took out two-- from two barracks, the children. And these people, for some reason, knew that their children went to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they asked us, where are you coming from? So, we tell them that we're from Czechoslovakia. They took us to Auschwitz. And we're coming now from Auschwitz. So they said, did you see children there? We didn't have a heart to tell them no. So we told them yes. We saw-- we saw children.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they were happy that somebody saw their children. And somebody is taking care of their children. And we came just-- all we had is one dress, no hair. So they gave us clothes. They gave us food. They-- they had, because they came in with their belongings to the ghetto. And then they divided them. The men were on one side, the women on the other side. And the children had two barracks. But they took out the children just before we came.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we asked them, what is here? What is this camp? What-- are we going to work here? So they said, yes. You're going to work. You've going to be assigned to some kind of work. I ask one woman, what are you-- we were able to communicate, because in Jewish. They spoke Jewish, and we spoke Jewish. I says, what-- what kind of work you do? So one says, I'm a seamstress. The other one says, I'm a shoemaker. So we weren't afraid of working as long-- as long we-- they leave us live. And we weren't afraid to-- for the work. At this point, did you still have hope that you might find your father and brothers alive. Or, you had given up hope?

speaker: interviewee
text: As much as we heard, we didn't want to believe it. We believed that they're alive. We still believe that my father. And we knew that if there is any chance they're going to give my father, he will survive. Because he was a person that was able to help himself and help other people. He was that kind of a person, that he was always like a leader. He knew how to go. He went through a lot. But he knew how to survive. So we were hoping that he will survive, definitely.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you continue to believe that he was alive?

speaker: interviewee
text: Even after we were liberated, we want to go home to see if my father's alive. So we always believed that maybe. And whenever we got together with people, we always asked, where are you from? Did you see my-- if they saw my father. Not for my brothers, but my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you in fact-- were you, in fact, assigned work in Plaszow?

speaker: interviewee
text: The next day, they took us out-- roll call, [NON-ENGLISH]. So when it came, it was finished, the [NON-ENGLISH], the people that were working, that were assigned to work, the Polish people, they went to work. And we Czechs remained on the [NON-ENGLISH]

speaker: interviewee
text: So somebody came, a kapo. They-- they named the people that were in charge on the work kapos. So a kapo came. And he wanted 200 girls. So he just counted 200 girls. We followed him.

speaker: interviewee
text: There were a few kapos that were in charge. So we came. We followed him. And-- and he took us. We walked, like, over a hill. And past that hill, there were ditches there. And we didn't know what those ditches are. We thought maybe that these ditches are-- they're going to kill us there. Because we heard about ditches, they kill people. So everything was in the dark. So he said us we should sit down, and we should wait there. And we sat down. And then, all of a sudden, we see trucks [INAUDIBLE] come-- coming. Trucks covered-- the trucks were covered. And they're coming up the hill and coming down. And the trucks stopped. And they-- there were also men that were there working, the Polish men. And they opened up. They already knew what to do, these Polish men. It was the first time for us, but these Polish men were already there working before. So they open up the trucks. And we saw people coming out from there-- these trucks. There were people, men and women, no children. But like middle age some were very young. Some were, like, in their 20's. Some were older, let's say. Not very old, sort of maybe 50 years. And they were very nice dressed. They were-- you saw that these people were better class people that they were dressed. They took them-- lined them-- they didn't line them up. They just took them from the trucks off. And they told-- one was a commandant. He said that this and this date, a solider-- they found a soldier on this and this street killed. And they took all these people political. And you have to pay with your life. And I says, oh, my god, they're going to kill these people here. They-- blind-- we put on-- they told us to blindfold the people, took off their jackets. They had raincoats. We took off their boots. We took as much clothes as we could.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they protesting when you did that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: The people, were they resisting when you did that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. He-- he-- they were just-- they didn't cry. They were just standing there. And I remember one girl held onto a boy. They were walking together. [SOBBING] And they told them to walk. And when they walked, they just fell into the ditch. [SOB] And the soldiers shot them. And they poured gasoline over them. And they burned them. I'm sure some of them weren't even dead. [CRYING] I-- I can't begin to tell you how hysterical we were. I said to my sister, I don't care if they kill me. This is the end. I used to so afraid of a dead person. Now, this-- to see somebody die, it was so horrible. So difficult. These people were-- they said they were Jewish people. They were from Krakau. They lived in Krakau. We knew that there is no more Jewish people in Krakau. When he came home, when he told the Polish people that were working there that-- what kind of work we're doing, so they said they know, because they'd been working there, too. And he says, before they were shooting there Jewish people from the camp. Now, they don't shoot anymore Jewish people. It became [INAUDIBLE] I don't know, [INAUDIBLE]. It became [INAUDIBLE]. They're not shooting Jewish people there.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I says, my god. If this is-- I just don't want to live. I couldn't eat. My sisters said, you have to eat. [SOB] I says, how can I eat? How can I-- I couldn't sleep. I couldn't eat. [SOBBING]

speaker: interviewee
text: I just saw these people walking there and-- and burning them. I-- I says-- I don't want to live. This is-- this is worse than dying. I just pray to God I should die.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the next day, we went to [NON-ENGLISH]. So the woman said-- one woman said, look. You know this kapo. He's going to come tomorrow again for people. Try to see if you could sneak away from this kapo. I says, how can we sneak away? We're standing there. He says, when you see that kapo comes, try to go in the back. So we did.

speaker: interviewee
text: And by luck, this kapo took other people to work. We didn't go any more there. We just worked one day there. But they took us also-- very, very hard work they gave us. They had-- this was like just punishing us. They had big boards. These boards were maybe 10 feet long. And they were heavy. And three people had to carry these boards. So my two sisters, they were taller. I was smaller.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they put me in the middle that I shouldn't have so much weight on me And the German shepherds were chasing us while we were carrying those boards. And whoever couldn't run fast enough, the German shepherds would tear them apart.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a girl there [SOBBING] that I knew from home. She was dying there, because the dogs bit her. And they put her on the side that we should see that if we're not gonna run fast, that's what's going to happen to us.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were working. It was called [NON-ENGLISH]. It was a punishment. I don't know why. We were punished, because we were Jews. So the Polish people-- the Jewish people there that were-- that were in that camp, they felt very bad for us. Because they saw how they're torturing these Jewish-- the new girls. So they used to put bread [? on the ?] [? road ?] we should have. Because the food there was impossible to eat.

speaker: interviewee
text: They used to cut the grass with daisies and cook it with the sand and give us. It was so bitter. And it was full of sand. We couldn't eat. But the Polish people used to help us, give us food, and bread, and clothes. And they felt sorry for us. But it didn't last long, because then some people came again. Another transport came from Auschwitz. These were Hungarian girls already. And these Polish people, Jewish people, asked the Hungarians if they still saw their children. And they said, we didn't see any children. And that was a mistake. So when they found out that we lied to them, they were angry at us. And they stopped helping us with food and clothing. And-- and it start-- it was like a divided-- we were Jewish. And they were Jewish, but they were against us. Then, we took away the children's place. It wasn't our doing. But still, they were very bitter.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they doing different work from you in the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were they doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were working in-- in places like sewing and shoemakers and kitchen. They were doing different kind of work. They were settled in-- in working place. We only worked in the [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: So one day also, it was [INAUDIBLE]. We knew the holidays. We didn't observe them, but we knew the holidays.

speaker: interviewee
text: On [INAUDIBLE], we were burning [INAUDIBLE]. They were the [INAUDIBLE] from Krakau shul. There were place-- this was a cemetery. Before the cemetery, there was a building. And they were there.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they made us made ditches and burn the [INAUDIBLE]. I never touched a [INAUDIBLE] in my life. And here, I had to take the [INAUDIBLE] and put it in a ditch and burn it. So the [INAUDIBLE] did all this kind of work. So and-- also, one day the-- a-- a kapo came, a young boy. And he took us. We never knew where we wound up to work. So he took us to work.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he-- he-- we had to throw big rocks from one to another lined up. And I would throw the rock to the next one. And if it fell, he had to hit us, this Jewish boy, the kapo. And he said, I'm not going to hit the Jewish boy-- girls. And the commandant was watching. So he says, if you're not going to hit them, I'm going to let my dog go on you. And he didn't want to hit us. And we begged him, please, hit us. He let the dog go on him. He bit him, the dog. And the boy died after. We were hysterical crying. Please. He says, I'm not going to hit Jewish girls. He refused to hit us. And the commandant let the dogs on him.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We're going to stop here to change tapes.
